title: What a young manager owes an older subordinate
summary: Explain how a manager in their thirties should treat a team member twice their age — neither pretending the seniority gap doesn't exist nor letting it quietly run the team.
topic: How authority and respect should be balanced when the person you manage is decades older than you
prompt: Write a 450–600-word explanatory essay on how a young manager should handle the relationship with a subordinate who is much older and more experienced — without either pretending the age gap is invisible or letting it undermine the manager's authority. Explain the principles, not just feelings, with two concrete examples and one fair objection. Use at least one cleft sentence, one passive construction, and cohesion devices such as 'in practice' or 'by contrast'.
hintStarters: The first time I had to give feedback to a man who had been doing the job since before I could read, I…
What an older subordinate resents is rarely the authority itself but the pretence that…
In practice, respect and direction are not opposites; the trick is to ask for one while still offering…
Granted, deferring too much to experience can quietly hand the older colleague a veto that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: nhường / tôn trọng ý kiến ai
thứ bậc / hệ thống cấp bậc
dùng chức vụ để áp đảo
hoài nghi / đoán già đoán non về quyết định của ai
sự ăn ý / mối quan hệ tốt
động lực quyền lực giữa các bên
chuỗi mệnh lệnh / hệ thống chỉ huy

sampleText:
The first time I had to give feedback to a man who had been doing the job since before I could read, I rehearsed the conversation for a week and still got it wrong. I softened every sentence until the message vanished, then watched him leave the room unsure whether he had been corrected or thanked. Managing someone much older than you is not, it turns out, a problem of courage. It is a problem of design — of working out, in advance, what you actually owe each other.

What an older subordinate resents is rarely the authority itself but the pretence that the age gap is invisible. The thirty-year-old who breezes in talking about 'shaking things up', as though two decades of accumulated knowledge were a glitch to be patched, will lose the room before the first meeting ends. By contrast, naming the obvious costs nothing and buys a great deal: a quiet acknowledgement that the person across the desk has seen more of this work than you have is not a surrender of authority. It is the precondition for being given any.

In practice, respect and direction are not opposites. The trick is to ask for the older colleague's judgement on the parts where experience genuinely matters, while still owning the decisions that are yours to make. Consider a team lead who inherited an engineer thirty years her senior. She did not pretend to know more about the legacy system than he did; instead, she asked him to design the migration and made it clear, warmly but plainly, that the deadline was hers to set. He kept the dignity of the expert; she kept the authority of the manager. Each was given what they were actually owed, and the arrangement held because neither had to lie about the other.

Now consider the failure mode. A different manager, terrified of seeming arrogant, deferred on everything, until the older employee was effectively running the team through him. Meetings were quietly steered, decisions were reopened weeks after they were closed, and junior staff learned to take their real questions to the senior man and their paperwork to the boss. Nothing was said outright. The authority had simply been handed over without ever being claimed.

Granted, deferring too much to experience can quietly hand the older colleague a veto that the org chart never granted them, and that risk is real. There are also people who will read any instruction from a younger boss as an insult, and no amount of tact will reach them. To pretend that every such relationship can be made comfortable is dishonest. Some cannot, and a manager who keeps apologising for their own role only deepens the resentment they hoped to avoid.

What the young manager finally owes, then, is neither deference nor performance of confidence, but clarity: a clear account of which decisions are shared and which are not, offered early and held to. It is that clarity, far more than charm or seniority, that lets two people separated by thirty years work as colleagues rather than as a quiet contest. The age gap does not need to be hidden, and it does not need to be won. It needs to be named once, fairly, and then mostly left alone.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences to isolate the real issue
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What an older subordinate resents is rarely the authority itself but the pretence …' (what-cleft) và 'It is that clarity … that lets two people … work as colleagues' (it-cleft) dùng câu chẻ để tách bạch điều cấp dưới thật sự oán giận và yếu tố thật sự giúp mối quan hệ vận hành.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What an older subordinate resents is rarely the authority itself but the pretence …' (what-cleft) and 'It is that clarity … that lets two people … work as colleagues' (it-cleft) use clefts to isolate what the subordinate truly resents and what truly makes the relationship work.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to expose authority slipping away unnoticed
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'decisions were reopened weeks after they were closed' và 'The authority had simply been handed over without ever being claimed' dùng bị động để cho thấy quyền hạn tuột đi mà không ai chủ động làm điều đó công khai, phản ánh sự thất bại âm thầm của người quản lý.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'decisions were reopened weeks after they were closed' and 'The authority had simply been handed over without ever being claimed' use the passive to show authority slipping away with no one openly doing it, mirroring the manager's quiet failure.

sampleTranslationVi:
Lần đầu tiên tôi phải đưa phản hồi cho một người đã làm công việc này từ trước khi tôi biết đọc, tôi đã tập dượt cuộc trò chuyện suốt một tuần mà vẫn làm hỏng. Tôi làm dịu từng câu cho đến khi thông điệp biến mất, rồi nhìn ông rời phòng mà không chắc mình vừa bị chỉnh sửa hay được cảm ơn. Hóa ra, quản lý một người lớn tuổi hơn bạn nhiều không phải là vấn đề về lòng can đảm. Đó là một vấn đề về thiết kế — về việc tìm ra, từ trước, điều bạn thực sự nợ nhau.

Cái mà một cấp dưới lớn tuổi oán giận hiếm khi là quyền hạn mà là sự giả vờ rằng khoảng cách tuổi tác là vô hình. Người ba mươi tuổi hùng hổ bước vào nói về chuyện 'làm mới mọi thứ', như thể hai thập kỷ kiến thức tích lũy là một lỗi cần được vá, sẽ mất lòng cả phòng trước khi cuộc họp đầu tiên kết thúc. Ngược lại, gọi tên điều hiển nhiên chẳng tốn gì mà mua được rất nhiều: một sự thừa nhận lặng lẽ rằng người ngồi bên kia bàn đã chứng kiến công việc này nhiều hơn bạn không phải là một sự đầu hàng quyền hạn. Đó là điều kiện tiên quyết để được trao bất kỳ quyền hạn nào.

Trên thực tế, sự tôn trọng và sự chỉ đạo không phải là hai mặt đối lập. Bí quyết là hỏi xin nhận định của đồng nghiệp lớn tuổi ở những phần mà kinh nghiệm thực sự quan trọng, trong khi vẫn nhận trách nhiệm về những quyết định thuộc về bạn. Hãy xét một trưởng nhóm thừa hưởng một kỹ sư hơn cô ba mươi tuổi. Cô không giả vờ hiểu về hệ thống cũ nhiều hơn anh; thay vào đó, cô nhờ anh thiết kế việc di trú dữ liệu và nói rõ, ấm áp nhưng thẳng thắn, rằng thời hạn là điều cô có quyền đặt ra. Anh giữ được phẩm giá của chuyên gia; cô giữ được quyền hạn của người quản lý. Mỗi người được trao đúng điều họ thực sự được hưởng, và sự sắp xếp ấy trụ vững bởi không ai phải nói dối về người kia.

Giờ hãy xét đến kiểu thất bại. Một người quản lý khác, sợ bị xem là kiêu ngạo, đã nhún nhường trong mọi việc, cho đến khi người nhân viên lớn tuổi trên thực tế đang điều hành cả nhóm thông qua anh ta. Các cuộc họp bị lái đi một cách lặng lẽ, các quyết định bị mở lại nhiều tuần sau khi đã được chốt, và nhân viên cấp dưới học cách mang những câu hỏi thật sự đến cho người đàn ông kỳ cựu còn giấy tờ thì đến cho sếp. Chẳng có gì được nói ra. Quyền hạn chỉ đơn giản là đã được trao đi mà chưa từng được nhận lấy.

Công nhận, nhún nhường quá nhiều trước kinh nghiệm có thể lặng lẽ trao cho đồng nghiệp lớn tuổi một quyền phủ quyết mà sơ đồ tổ chức chưa bao giờ cấp cho họ, và rủi ro đó là có thật. Cũng có những người sẽ hiểu bất kỳ chỉ thị nào từ một vị sếp trẻ tuổi như một sự xúc phạm, và không sự khéo léo nào chạm tới được họ. Giả vờ rằng mọi mối quan hệ như thế đều có thể trở nên dễ chịu là không trung thực. Một số thì không thể, và một người quản lý cứ liên tục xin lỗi vì vai trò của chính mình chỉ làm sâu thêm nỗi oán giận mà họ mong tránh.

Vậy thì điều người quản lý trẻ rốt cuộc nợ không phải sự nhún nhường cũng chẳng phải màn diễn tự tin, mà là sự rõ ràng: một lời giải thích rõ ràng về việc quyết định nào được chia sẻ và quyết định nào thì không, được đưa ra sớm và được giữ vững. Chính sự rõ ràng đó, hơn nhiều so với sức hút hay thâm niên, mới cho phép hai con người cách nhau ba mươi tuổi làm việc như đồng nghiệp thay vì như một cuộc đấu thầm lặng. Khoảng cách tuổi tác không cần phải được che giấu, và cũng không cần phải được chiến thắng. Nó cần được gọi tên một lần, một cách công bằng, rồi phần lớn để yên.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to isolate what the older subordinate truly resents?
options: An older subordinate resents what is rarely the authority but the pretence.
The pretence is what an older the authority subordinate resents rarely itself.
Resents an older subordinate what rarely the pretence is authority itself.
What an older subordinate resents is rarely the authority itself but the pretence that the age gap is invisible.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X resents is rarely A but B' is a what-cleft foregrounding the pretence rather than the authority.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What an older subordinate resents is …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to name what makes the relationship work?
options: It is that clarity, far more than charm or seniority, that lets two people separated by thirty years work as colleagues.
That clarity lets two people work as colleagues which is more than charm.
Clarity it is far more than charm that two people work seniority colleagues.
Work as colleagues two people that clarity it is far charm than more.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X that …' is an it-cleft spotlighting clarity as the decisive factor.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is that clarity … that lets …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to show authority slipping away unnoticed?
options: He simply handed over the authority and never claimed any of it himself.
The authority had simply been handed over without ever being claimed.
Handing over the authority is what claiming none of it does to him.
Claimed never being authority the handed over had simply without it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had been handed over … without ever being claimed' is passive, hiding the doer to show authority drifting away.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses passive forms ('been handed over', 'being claimed')?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to defer to someone' mean as the essay uses it?
options: To postpone a meeting with them
To report them to a higher authority
To yield to their judgement out of respect
To refuse to speak to them
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Defer to' means to give way to someone's opinion or expertise.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of letting the more experienced person's view prevail.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option concedes the objection fairly and in the essay's register?
options: Older workers simply can't accept a younger boss and that's their fault.
Obviously experience is worthless and should be ignored entirely by managers.
Of course any older employee who objects is just being difficult on purpose.
Granted, deferring too much to experience can quietly hand the older colleague a veto that the org chart never granted them.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' admits a real risk of over-deference without abandoning the argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option acknowledges a genuine downside of deferring too much?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges its claim appropriately rather than overstating?
options: There are also people who will read any instruction from a younger boss as an insult, and no amount of tact will reach them.
Every older employee always resents a younger boss without exception.
No older employee ever has any problem with a younger boss at all.
Read an insult any instruction will people who from a younger boss as.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'There are also people who …' limits the claim to some, avoiding 'every … always' and 'no … ever'.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid the absolutes; choose the option that says 'some' people.

mcQuestions.prompt: In the successful example, how did the team lead balance respect and authority?
options: She pretended to know the legacy system better than the engineer
She asked the engineer to design the migration but kept the deadline as hers to set
She let the engineer set all the deadlines himself
She removed the engineer from the project entirely
mcQuestions.explanation: She gave him the expert's role while keeping the manager's decision, so each kept what they were owed.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the paragraph about the legacy system.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent explanatory register?
options: Honestly older coworkers can be a total nightmare and you just gotta wing it.
Basically just be chill and hope the old guy doesn't get salty about it.
Respect and direction are not opposites; the trick is to ask for one while still owning the other.
It's kinda awkward, you know, bossing around someone old enough to be your dad.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the principle precisely; the others slip into casual speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which cohesion device best contrasts the failure mode with the good approach?
options: In conclusion, deferring is always the safest choice.
Anyway, none of these distinctions really matter in practice.
On the contrary, experience should always override the manager.
By contrast, naming the obvious costs nothing and buys a great deal.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'By contrast, …' cleanly sets the open approach against the manager who 'shakes things up'.
mcQuestions.hint: Which connector signals a deliberate contrast between two behaviours?

options: To use one's higher position to force a decision or override someone
To resign from a position
To organise a team by seniority
To ask a senior colleague for advice
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Pull rank' means to assert authority by virtue of one's position.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of saying 'because I'm the boss' to end a discussion.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most cleanly?
options: He kept the dignity of the expert; the authority of the manager being kept by her.
He kept the dignity of the expert; she kept the authority of the manager.
Keeping the dignity expert he; she the manager authority of to keep.
The expert dignity he kept of; her the manager authority kept of she.
mcQuestions.explanation: Two matched clauses ('He kept … ; she kept …') form a clean parallel; the others break the pattern.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option mirrors the two clauses in the same shape?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say the young manager finally owes the older subordinate?
options: Constant deference to their experience
A confident performance that hides any doubt
Clarity about which decisions are shared and which are not
An apology for being younger
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion names 'clarity', not deference or performance, as what is owed.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the final paragraph's opening sentence.

options: To manage a very small team
To delegate all work to others
To manage from a remote location
To control every small detail of others' work too closely
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Micromanage' means supervising work in excessive, controlling detail.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a boss who checks every tiny step.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's balanced conclusion?
options: The age gap does not need to be hidden or won; it needs to be named once, fairly, and then mostly left alone.
The age gap should be hidden so no one ever mentions it.
The age gap should be won decisively by pulling rank early.
The age gap means the older employee should always be in charge.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion rejects both hiding and 'winning' the gap, favouring naming it once and moving on.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that avoids both extremes.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the em-dash insertion correctly to add a defining aside?
options: It is a problem — of design of working out in — advance what owe you each.
It is a problem of design — of working out, in advance, what you actually owe each other.
Of design it is — a problem working out — in advance what each other owe.
Working out a problem — it is of design — in advance you each other owe what.
mcQuestions.explanation: The dash introduces a clarifying restatement; the main clause stands alone without it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets you delete the dashed part and keep a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'condescending' mean as used here?
options: Generous and kind
Highly experienced
Treating someone as if they are less capable or important than you
Quiet and reserved
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Condescending' describes a patronising attitude that looks down on someone.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of talking down to someone.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a time you had authority over someone older or more experienced than you — or watched it happen. Was the age gap ever named out loud, and did naming it (or refusing to) make the working relationship easier or harder?
